past_medical_history: Migraine without aura, diagnosed 10 years ago, episodic, 2-3 attacks per month
Mild intermittent low back pain, attributed to sedentary occupation
family_history: Father with hypertension and coronary artery disease, MI at age 62
No family history of stroke, connective tissue disorders, or early vascular events

social_history.smoking: Active smoker, 20 pack-year history (1 pack per day for 20 years)
social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinker, 2-3 beers per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Minimal regular exercise, occasional weekend cycling

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent with normal comprehension, repetition, and naming. No dysarthria. Attention is intact — performs serial sevens without error. Fund of knowledge appropriate.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Left pupil 2 mm, reactive to light. Right pupil 4 mm, reactive to light. Anisocoria more prominent in dim lighting. Left partial ptosis (2 mm palpebral fissure difference). Subtle left enophthalmos noted. No facial anhidrosis. Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No diplopia. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all three trigeminal divisions bilaterally. Mild left facial weakness is questionable — symmetric nasolabial folds, symmetric smile. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 strength bilaterally in deltoid, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, wrist flexors, grip, and finger abduction. Lower extremities: 5/5 strength bilaterally in hip flexion, knee extension, knee flexion, ankle dorsiflexion, ankle plantarflexion, and toe extension. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. No sensory extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 2+, Achilles 2+ bilaterally and symmetric. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait, tandem gait intact, Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 0. Pharmacologic pupil testing with apraclonidine 0.5% not yet performed. No carotid bruits. No meningismus. No Marfan habitus or hypermobility. Patient complains of a dull aching pain behind the left eye and mild left-sided neck discomfort.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of pain behind the left eye and drooping of the left eyelid during physical exertion
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Kemal Arslan is a 41-year-old right-handed man with a history of migraine without aura and a significant smoking history who presents to the emergency department after developing sudden onset of a sharp, severe pain behind his left eye approximately three hours ago. The pain began abruptly during vigorous physical exertion and was immediately followed by the sensation that his left eyelid was 'drooping' and that his left eye appeared 'smaller' than usual. His wife confirmed that the left pupil appeared smaller and the eyelid was partially closed. The pain has since evolved into a dull, persistent ache localized behind the left eye and extending into the left side of the neck. He rates the pain as 6 out of 10 at present, down from 8 out of 10 at onset. He denies any visual changes including blurred vision, double vision, or loss of visual field. He denies any limb weakness, numbness, tingling, difficulty speaking, or difficulty swallowing. There has been no headache with aura features, photophobia, phonophobia, or nausea, and the patient states this pain is distinctly different from his usual migraines, which he describes as bilateral throbbing headaches without any eye or eyelid involvement. There was no head or neck trauma preceding the event. He did not perform any unusual neck movements or chiropractic manipulation. He has not had any recent upper respiratory tract infection. He denies any palpitations, chest pain, shortness of breath, or syncope. He has never had similar symptoms before. He has been in his usual state of health otherwise. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes per day for the past twenty years and uses sumatriptan occasionally for migraines. He denies illicit drug use. He drove himself to the ED against his wife's wishes, as he initially thought the symptoms were just another migraine. On further questioning, he reports mild intermittent tinnitus on the left side since the event began, which he attributes to anxiety.

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery, extending from the cervical segment (C1) to the petrous segment (C2)
findings.size: Crescent-shaped signal abnormality spanning approximately 4.5 cm in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T1_fat_sat: Hyperintense crescent-shaped signal surrounding the left ICA lumen, consistent with subacute intramural hematoma (methemoglobin)
signal_characteristics.T2: Mixed signal intensity within the vessel wall, predominantly hyperintense
signal_characteristics.DWI: No acute restricted diffusion in the brain parenchyma
signal_characteristics.ADC: No corresponding hypointensity in the brain parenchyma

findings.mass_effect: Narrowing of the left ICA lumen due to intramural hematoma

additional_observations: MRA of the circle of Willis: Focal moderate narrowing of the left ICA at the C1 and C2 segments with reduced flow signal compared to the right ICA. String sign appearance noted in the cervical segment.
Right ICA, bilateral ACAs, bilateral MCAs, and posterior circulation vessels are patent with normal flow signal
No evidence of fibromuscular dysplasia in the visualized segments of the renal or other vessels
Mild disc desiccation at C5-C6 without canal stenosis
mri.impression: MRA demonstrates focal narrowing (string sign) of the left ICA at C1-C2 segments secondary to dissection. No acute ischemic infarction on DWI — no evidence of completed stroke at this time. Incidental right maxillary sinus retention cyst. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: No evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy
Early repolarization pattern in leads V2-V3 — normal variant in young male
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 84 bpm. Normal intervals. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Glucose 108 mg/dL (H), Total Cholesterol 218 mg/dL (H), LDL 142 mg/dL (H), Triglycerides 170 mg/dL (H), TSH 5.2 mIU/L (H).

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery, extending from 2 cm distal to the bifurcation to the skull base (petrous segment)

signal_characteristics.CTA: Eccentric mural thickening with intimal flap visualized in the cervical left ICA. Narrowed true lumen with crescent-shaped mural hematoma. String sign appearance of the distal cervical ICA. No complete occlusion — residual flow through the narrowed lumen. No pseudoaneurysm formation.
Normal caliber and wall thickness. No stenosis, dissection, or aneurysm.

additional_observations: Right ICA, bilateral vertebral arteries, and basilar artery are normal
Bilateral external carotid arteries are patent
No evidence of fibromuscular dysplasia in the cervical or intracranial vasculature
Intracranial circulation is intact with good collateral flow via the circle of Willis
Anterior communicating artery and bilateral posterior communicating arteries are patent, providing potential collateral pathways
output.impression: No complete occlusion. Good collateral circulation via the circle of Willis. No contralateral dissection, fibromuscular dysplasia, or other vasculopathy. Clinical correlation recommended.

signal_characteristics.Plaque_morphology: No atherosclerotic plaque. Vessel wall thickening with hypoechoic mural hematoma. Tapering narrowing of the lumen in the distal cervical ICA consistent with dissection.

additional_observations: Bilateral common carotid arteries demonstrate normal intima-media thickness for age
Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with antegrade flow and normal velocities
output.impression: Mural hematoma visible as hypoechoic vessel wall thickening. Right ICA and bilateral vertebral arteries are normal. No evidence of atherosclerotic disease bilaterally. Clinical correlation recommended.

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: All valves morphologically and functionally normal, no regurgitation or stenosis
No patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect on color Doppler. Bubble study performed — no right-to-left shunt at rest or with Valsalva.

findings: Rare premature atrial complexes (12 in 24 hours)
output.interpretation: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring reveals continuous normal sinus rhythm without any arrhythmias. Rare PACs are a normal finding. No atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other supraventricular tachycardia detected.

output.query: carotid artery dissection antithrombotic therapy antiplatelet anticoagulation management

results.title: Antiplatelet Versus Anticoagulation in Cervical Artery Dissection (CADISS Trial)
results.authors: Markus HS, Hayter E, Levi C, et al.
results.key_finding: No significant difference in stroke recurrence between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy in cervical artery dissection. Recurrent stroke rate was low in both groups (2% at 3 months). Antiplatelet therapy is generally preferred due to lower bleeding risk.

results.title: Cervical Artery Dissection: Diagnosis, Management, and Natural History (Review)
results.key_finding: Spontaneous cervicoarterial dissection accounts for approximately 2% of all ischemic strokes but up to 10-25% of strokes in young and middle-aged adults. Most dissections heal spontaneously within 3-6 months. Antithrombotic therapy is recommended for at least 3-6 months.

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guidelines: Management of Cervical Artery Dissection
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: For patients with ischemic stroke or TIA attributable to extracranial carotid or vertebral artery dissection, antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) for at least 3-6 months is reasonable. The choice between antiplatelet and anticoagulation should be individualized.

output.summary: Cervical artery dissection is a leading cause of stroke in young adults. The CADISS trial (2015) demonstrated no significant difference in efficacy between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy, with recurrent stroke rates of approximately 2% in both groups. Current AHA/ASA guidelines recommend antithrombotic therapy for at least 3-6 months. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin or clopidogrel) is generally preferred as first-line due to a more favorable safety profile. Follow-up vascular imaging at 3-6 months is recommended to assess for recanalization, residual stenosis, or pseudoaneurysm formation. Most dissections show significant improvement or complete resolution on follow-up imaging.

output.proposed: Aspirin 325 mg daily followed by transition to aspirin 81 mg daily
interactions: Ibuprofen (current medication): Concurrent use of ibuprofen and aspirin reduces the antiplatelet effect of aspirin. Ibuprofen should be discontinued and replaced with acetaminophen for pain management.

Sumatriptan (current medication): Theoretical increased risk of serotonin syndrome with concurrent antiplatelet agents is minimal. However, sumatriptan is a 5-HT1B/1D agonist that causes vasoconstriction and is CONTRAINDICATED in the setting of carotid dissection due to risk of vasospasm in a compromised vessel.
contraindications: Sumatriptan is contraindicated in the setting of carotid artery dissection — the vasoconstrictive mechanism may worsen vessel narrowing and increase stroke risk. All triptans should be avoided.
NSAIDs (ibuprofen) should be discontinued due to antiplatelet interaction and bleeding risk
warnings: Patient should be counseled to avoid triptans for migraine management and transition to non-vasoconstrictive alternatives (e.g., acetaminophen, gepants, or preventive therapy)
Avoid anticoagulants combined with antiplatelet therapy unless specifically indicated
output.formulary_status: Aspirin is formulary for antithrombotic therapy in cervical artery dissection
alternatives: Anticoagulation with apixaban 5 mg BID or warfarin (INR 2-3) if anticoagulation is preferred over antiplatelet
For migraine: rimegepant 75 mg (CGRP antagonist, non-vasoconstrictive) or preventive therapy with propranolol or amitriptyline

output.query: connective tissue disorder screening cervical artery dissection Ehlers-Danlos fibromuscular dysplasia Marfan

results.title: Connective Tissue Disorders and Cervical Artery Dissections
results.key_finding: While connective tissue disorders such as vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (type IV) are associated with arterial dissections, they account for only a small minority of cases. Most spontaneous cervical artery dissections occur in patients without identifiable connective tissue disorders.

results.title: Association of Fibromuscular Dysplasia and Cervical Artery Dissection
results.authors: Olin JW, Froehlich J, et al.
results.key_finding: Fibromuscular dysplasia is found in up to 15-20% of patients with cervical artery dissection. CTA or MRA screening of renal and iliac arteries should be considered. FMD is a multisystemic vascular disease that may predispose to dissection.

output.summary: Connective tissue disorders (vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome) and fibromuscular dysplasia are associated with cervical artery dissection but account for a minority of cases. Most spontaneous dissections are idiopathic, with potential contributions from minor trauma, infection, or genetic predisposition. Screening should include clinical assessment for joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and family history of vascular events. CTA/MRA of renal arteries may identify FMD. Genetic testing for vascular EDS (COL3A1 gene) should be reserved for patients with suggestive clinical features.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Left internal carotid artery dissection presenting with painful Horner syndrome, without completed ischemic stroke

differential.diagnosis: Cluster headache with associated autonomic features
differential.key_distinguishing: Cluster headache causes periorbital pain with ipsilateral miosis and ptosis, but pain is typically excruciating with restlessness, lacrimation, and rhinorrhea; episodes are shorter (15-180 minutes) and cluster in bouts; imaging would not show vascular abnormality

Patient has migraine history, but presentation is distinctly different from typical migraines; absence of photophobia, phonophobia, and nausea; ipsilateral Horner syndrome is not a feature of migraine; MRI findings are diagnostic

differential.diagnosis: Cavernous sinus lesion (tumor, thrombosis, or fistula)
differential.key_distinguishing: Cavernous sinus pathology typically involves cranial nerves III, IV, V1, V2, and VI with ophthalmoplegia; isolated Horner syndrome without extraocular movement abnormalities argues against this; MRI localized pathology to ICA, not cavernous sinus

differential.diagnosis: Pancoast tumor (pulmonary apex tumor causing preganglionic Horner syndrome)
differential.key_distinguishing: Would present with shoulder-arm pain, brachial plexopathy, and chest findings; no chest or upper extremity symptoms; the cervical ICA location of the lesion on MRI excludes a preganglionic sympathetic chain lesion

differential.diagnosis: Central Horner syndrome (brainstem lesion such as lateral medullary syndrome)
differential.key_distinguishing: Central Horner syndrome would be accompanied by other brainstem signs (dysphagia, ataxia, crossed sensory findings); isolated Horner syndrome without other neurological deficits favors a postganglionic or third-order neuron lesion; MRI shows no brainstem pathology

optimal_actions.action: Perform focused neurological examination identifying Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis, enophthalmos) and characterize the periorbital pain and neck pain
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left partial ptosis, miosis, enophthalmos — classic Horner syndrome triad without anhidrosis, suggesting postganglionic (third-order neuron) lesion

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI to evaluate for acute ischemic stroke, and MRI of the neck with T1 fat-saturated sequences to evaluate the carotid arteries
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No acute DWI lesion (no completed stroke), but T1 fat-sat hyperintense crescent around left ICA indicating intramural hematoma — diagnostic of ICA dissection

optimal_actions.action: Obtain MR angiography or CT angiography of the head and neck to characterize the vascular abnormality
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Focal narrowing (string sign) of the left ICA from cervical to petrous segments, confirming dissection

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and vasculitis markers (ESR, CRP, ANA, ANCA)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC, BMP, coagulation, and inflammatory markers — rules out vasculitis; incidental findings of dyslipidemia and mildly elevated TSH

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for cardioembolic sources
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm, no arrhythmia — no cardioembolic source

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation) to prevent thromboembolic stroke from the dissection
Discontinue sumatriptan — triptans are contraindicated in the setting of carotid dissection due to vasoconstrictive mechanism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sumatriptan contraindicated with carotid dissection; ibuprofen interaction with aspirin identified

optimal_actions.action: Order echocardiogram to rule out cardioembolic source and assess for aortic root dilation suggestive of connective tissue disorder
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal echocardiogram, no PFO, normal aortic root — no cardioembolic source or connective tissue disorder features

optimal_actions.action: Admit patient for monitoring — although no stroke has occurred, the dissected ICA poses ongoing thromboembolic risk
Plan follow-up MRI/MRA in 3-6 months to assess for vessel recanalization and counsel on smoking cessation, blood pressure management, and avoidance of neck manipulation

critical_actions: Recognize Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis, enophthalmos) as a localizing sign pointing to the sympathetic chain along the ICA, not merely an autonomic variant of headache
Obtain MRI with T1 fat-saturated sequences and MRA to diagnose ICA dissection — the crescent sign of intramural hematoma is pathognomonic
Differentiate painful Horner syndrome of ICA dissection from cluster headache or migraine, which require different management
Initiate antithrombotic therapy promptly to prevent thromboembolic stroke from the dissected segment
Discontinue sumatriptan (triptan class) due to vasoconstrictive contraindication in the setting of carotid artery dissection
Rule out systemic vasculitis with inflammatory markers, as treatment would differ significantly
contraindicated_actions: Administering sumatriptan or any triptan for the headache — vasoconstrictive agents are contraindicated with carotid dissection and may precipitate ischemic stroke
Dismissing the presentation as a migraine or cluster headache without vascular imaging — missed ICA dissection carries high stroke risk
Performing neck manipulation or chiropractic adjustment — may worsen the dissection
Administering IV thrombolysis — there is no completed ischemic stroke (DWI negative) and the mechanism is dissection, not thromboembolism amenable to tPA
Discharging without antithrombotic therapy or adequate follow-up vascular imaging
key_reasoning_points: Painful Horner syndrome (ipsilateral periorbital pain with miosis, ptosis, and enophthalmos without anhidrosis) is the classic presentation of ICA dissection — the absence of facial anhidrosis localizes the sympathetic disruption to the postganglionic third-order neuron along the ICA
The onset during vigorous physical exertion (Valsalva-like strain) is a recognized trigger for spontaneous carotid dissection, even without direct neck trauma
Central Horner syndrome was excluded because no concomitant brainstem findings were present on examination
Preganglionic Horner syndrome (e.g., Pancoast tumor) was excluded by the absence of chest, shoulder, or arm symptoms
Normal inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP, ANA, ANCA) effectively rule out vasculitis as the cause of ICA narrowing
The T1 fat-saturated MRI showing hyperintense crescent around the ICA lumen is diagnostic of intramural hematoma, confirming dissection
Absence of DWI restriction is a favorable prognostic sign — the dissection has caused Horner syndrome (sympathetic chain compression) but has not yet resulted in thromboembolic stroke

The mildly elevated TSH and dyslipidemia are incidental findings that serve as confounders — they are not etiologically related to the dissection but may draw attention away from the primary diagnosis

Migraine history is a red herring — while migraines are loosely associated with cervical artery dissection in epidemiological studies, this presentation is clinically distinct from the patient's typical migraines

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate — presentation of painful Horner syndrome mimics cluster headache or migraine with autonomic features. Patient's migraine history serves as a red herring. Incidental lab findings (elevated TSH, dyslipidemia) add confounders. The key diagnostic evidence is in the MRI tool output (T1 fat-sat crescent sign), requiring the agent to recognize Horner syndrome as a localizing sign for ICA pathology rather than dismissing it as a primary headache disorder.
